text: RUD (rapid unscheduled disassembly) events are not unusual in the rocket world https://t.co/zMc7x0A5Em
summary: RUD (rapid unscheduled disassembly) events are not unusual in the rocket world
ai_summary: Elon Musk addresses a SpaceX rocket failure (rapid unscheduled disassembly/explosion) by contextualizing it as a normal part of rocket development, not an anomaly. This reflects a critical leadership and engineering philosophy: normalizing failure as part of the iterative development process is essential for pushing technological boundaries and achieving breakthrough innovation in aerospace. The insight applies to SpaceX's development strategy and broader lessons about acceptable risk tolerance when building transformative technology.
embed_html:
RUD (rapid unscheduled disassembly) events are not unusual in the rocket world https://t.co/zMc7x0A5Em

— Elon Musk (@elonmusk) May 29, 2026

parent.text: BREAKING: Massive explosion by Blue Origin's New Glenn rocket on a launchpad in Cape Canaveral, Florida Major setback for Jeff Bezos
parent.name: Visegrád 24
parent.embed_html:
BREAKING:

Massive explosion by Blue Origin's New Glenn rocket on a launchpad in Cape Canaveral, Florida

Major setback for Jeff Bezos pic.twitter.com/qTOpDxGXLB

— Visegrád 24 (@visegrad24) May 29, 2026

ai_summary: Blue Origin's New Glenn rocket experienced a major launch failure at Cape Canaveral, prompting Elon Musk's response that 'rockets are hard'—acknowledging the extreme technical and engineering challenges in space launch development. This illustrates a core operational reality for aerospace companies: rocket development involves massive financial risk, technical complexity, and high failure rates, serving as a reminder of the difficulty in executing space exploration at scale, directly relevant to SpaceX's own launch operations and the broader commercial space industry.
embed_html:
Most unfortunate. Rockets are hard.

— Elon Musk (@elonmusk) May 29, 2026

text: @blueorigin @StephenClark1 Sorry to see this, I hope you recover quickly

parent.text: We experienced an anomaly during today's hotfire test. All personnel have been accounted for. We will provide updates as we learn more.
parent.name: Blue Origin
parent.embed_html:
We experienced an anomaly during today's hotfire test. All personnel have been accounted for. We will provide updates as we learn more.

— Blue Origin (@blueorigin) May 29, 2026

ai_summary: Blue Origin experienced an anomaly during a rocket engine hotfire test, with all personnel safely accounted for. Elon Musk's response—offering condolences and wishing for a quick recovery—illustrates professional courtesy in the highly competitive space industry, while the incident itself underscores the inherent technical risks and engineering challenges involved in developing rocket propulsion systems for commercial spaceflight.
embed_html:
Sorry to see this, I hope you recover quickly

— Elon Musk (@elonmusk) May 29, 2026

parent.embed_html:
I interview dozens/hundreds of new grads, nearly every day of the year. These are people with a well-formatted resume and a Bachelor's Degree in Engineering from well-regarded US universities and a GPA above 3.6. The majority cannot engineer, cannot function independently, cannot… https://t.co/4ag6dzMxTS

— Casey Handmer (@CJHandmer) May 28, 2026

ai_summary: CJHandmer reports a critical hiring problem: top-tier engineering graduates (3.6+ GPA from respected universities) often lack practical engineering capability, independence, and functional competency despite strong credentials. This highlights a systemic gap between academic credentials and real-world engineering execution that directly impacts tech companies' ability to scale through hiring, affecting organizations like Tesla, SpaceX, and other engineering-intensive businesses that rely on fresh talent pipelines.
embed_html:
Major problem

— Elon Musk (@elonmusk) May 29, 2026
